policy_statement: By 2030, establish public-private cooperation mechanisms to execute adaptation actions at national and local scale (A6b).
mechanism_description: Establishes public-private cooperation mechanisms to support the execution of adaptation actions at national and local levels, improving institutional capacity to adapt to climate impacts.
primary_causal_pathway: Governance cooperation → ↑ institutional capacity/coordination → ↑ implementation of risk-reduction actions → ↓ climate hazard exposure & vulnerability
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Human/system change: Government creates formal public–private cooperation mechanisms (roles, partnerships, coordination, financing/implementation arrangements) for adaptation delivery across scales. (2) Mediating process: Improved institutional capacity—better coordination, resource mobilization, information sharing, and execution capability—reduces implementation barriers and speeds/expands deployment of adaptation measures. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Greater and more effective adaptation implementation reduces vulnerability and exposure to climate impacts (e.g., heat, drought, floods), lowering damages and disruption.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improved ability to implement measures that reduce exposure and vulnerability to climate hazards (i.e., increased adaptive capacity leading to lower climate impacts).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Governance cooperation increases institutional adaptive capacity, which enables actions that reduce climate risk (exposure/vulnerability).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While cooperation could incidentally support mitigation (e.g., enabling low-carbon projects), the mechanism as defined targets adaptation execution capacity; the most direct and largest climate effect is reduced climate impacts via improved adaptive capacity.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific cooperation mechanism were explicitly created to deliver emissions-reduction projects (e.g., renewable procurement PPPs), that would be a different mechanism and could shift to Mitigation; under this canonical mechanism (adaptive capacity), the dominant pathway remains adaptation.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that establishes public–private cooperation mechanisms explicitly to execute adaptation actions is fundamentally about adaptive capacity and risk reduction, fitting Adaptation across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any finer subclassification (e.g., governance/finance/implementation capacity; specific hazards addressed) depends on policy-level evidence and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be misclassified only if “cooperation mechanisms” are primarily intended for mitigation delivery rather than adaptation; confirm intent (adaptation execution vs emissions reduction) in ambiguous cases.
key_indicators: “By 2030”; “establish”; “public-private cooperation mechanisms”; “execution of adaptation actions”; “national and local scale”
co_benefits: Improved general service delivery and investment coordination beyond climate adaptation
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a procurement/PPP framework, but the text does not specify procurement requirements or financing terms—only the establishment of cooperation mechanisms—so planning/strategy is the best fit.
